label: ai
topic: Satirical argument that university student government should be replaced with a monarchy
text:
Every spring, like clockwork, our university resurrects the same tired ritual: student government elections. Flyers bloom across campus like an invasive species, candidates suddenly remember how to smile in public, and everyone pretends—just for a week—that this whole system actually matters. It's hard not to admire the sheer theatrical commitment. We simulate democracy with all the enthusiasm of a middle school mock trial, except the stakes somehow feel even lower. And yet we keep doing it. Which is why I'd like to propose something bold, something honest, something that finally reflects the reality of our political ecosystem: abolish student government entirely and replace it with an absolute monarchy.

Hear me out. If we're going to pretend power exists, we might as well concentrate it into one gloriously unaccountable figurehead instead of distributing it among committees that debate the font size of funding proposals for three hours. Thomas Hobbes argued that life without a strong sovereign devolves into chaos, a "war of all against all." I'm not saying our student government is exactly that, but if you've ever watched a budget meeting spiral into passive-aggressive email chains and procedural deadlock, you might start to see his point. At least Hobbes had the decency to admit that people are messy and self-interested; our current system pretends that a group of sleep-deprived juniors can transcend that with Robert's Rules of Order.

An absolute monarch, on the other hand, would cut through the nonsense. No more campaigning on vague promises like "increasing transparency" or "amplifying student voices," phrases so overused they've lost all semantic content. Instead, we would have a sovereign—let's call them the Supreme Chancellor of Campus—who rules by decree. Dining hall hours? Extended or shortened at a whim. Club funding? Granted based on royal favor, which, frankly, is probably less arbitrary than the current rubric. Parking enforcement? Abolished on days when the monarch feels benevolent, ruthlessly enforced when they wake up on the wrong side of the dorm.

Of course, a monarchy requires appropriate privileges. The Supreme Chancellor should have exclusive access to the best study rooms, permanently reserved under the justification of "state business." They should be carried to class in a sedan chair by volunteers seeking favor—an excellent leadership development opportunity, if you ask me. Finals week could include a ceremonial "Royal Curve," where grades are adjusted according to how amused the monarch was by your coursework. And once a semester, we hold a grand banquet in their honor, featuring dining hall food that, for one night only, is actually edible.

You might object that this sounds absurd, and you'd be right. But I'd argue it's no more absurd than our current illusion of participatory governance. Voter turnout hovers somewhere between "statistically negligible" and "did we even send out the email?" Candidates run unopposed, or worse, they run on platforms so indistinguishable that choosing between them feels like picking a favorite grain of rice. The entire process is a pageant of earnestness layered over indifference. At least a monarchy would drop the pretense. It would be honest about power being concentrated, decisions being arbitrary, and most students not paying attention.

There's also something refreshingly unifying about having a single figure to blame. Right now, when something goes wrong, responsibility diffuses into a bureaucratic fog. Was it the senate? The executive board? Some subcommittee that meets at 8 a.m. on Tuesdays? With a monarch, there's no confusion. The Supreme Chancellor is either beloved or despised, sometimes both in the same week. It gives campus discourse a clarity it sorely lacks.

Ultimately, this proposal is less about actually crowning someone and more about confronting the gap between what student government claims to be and what it actually is. We dress up a largely symbolic institution in the language of democracy and then act surprised when no one takes it seriously. Maybe the real satire is that we keep pretending otherwise. If we're going to engage in political theater, we might as well make it entertaining. Long live the monarch—may their reign be as arbitrary as our current system, but infinitely more honest.
